item.title: Linux Desktop Share Tops 6% In 15 Million-System Analysis
item.author: BeauHD
item.content:encoded: An anonymous reader quotes a report from ZDNet: In an interview, Lansweeper, an IT asset discovery and inventory company, revealed to ZDNET that, in its analysis of over 15 million identified consumer desktop operating systems, it found that Linux desktops currently account for just over 6% of PC market share. This news comes after several other studies have shown the Linux desktop is right around the 6% mark. Indeed, according to the US Federal Government Website and App Analytics count, the Linux desktop market share over the last 90 days has reached 6.3%, a new high. In July, according to StatCounter, the Linux desktop also set a record high by its metrics with 5.24%.

item.title: zabbix >= 7.4.1-2 may require manual intervention
item.author: Robin Candau
item.content:encoded: Starting with , the following Zabbix system user accounts (previously shipped by their related packages) will no longer be used. Instead, all Zabbix components will now rely on a shared user account (as originally intended by upstream and done by other distributions):zabbix-agent (also used by the package)This shared user account is provided by the newly introduced package, which is now a dependency for all relevant packages.The switch to the new user account is handled automatically for the corresponding main configuration files and service units.However, manual intervention may be required if you created custom files or configurations referencing to and / or being owned by the above deprecated users accounts, for example: files used for encrypted communicationCustom scripts for metrics collections or report generations rules for metrics requiring elevated privileges to be collectedThose should therefore be updated to refer to and / or be owned by the new user account, otherwise some services or user parameters may fail to work properly, or not at all.

item.title: July GNU Spotlight with Amin Bandali featuring seventeen new GNU releases: Bash, Cflow, and more!
item.content:encoded: Seventeen new GNU releases in the last month (as of July 31, 2025).

item.title: 626: The Btrfs Blues
item.author: Jupiter Broadcasting
item.content:encoded: A Btrfs bug that bites is in the wild, and we discover whole home audio that works like a charm.
